options.text: I'm going to call her tonight.
She'll be here soon.
We will going to leave at six.
Are you going to help me?

questions.explanation: The error is (c): you can't stack two futures — say either "We are going to leave" or "We will leave", never "will going to". (a), (b) and (d) are all correct (going-to plan, will prediction, going-to question).

questions.prompt: Put the words in the correct order: (you / are / what / going / do / to)

options.text: What are going you to do?
What are you going to do?
What you are going to do?
What going to you are do?

questions.explanation: A going-to question is: question word + be + subject + going to + base verb → What are you going to do? "What you are going to do?" keeps statement order (no inversion), and the other two scramble be, the subject and going to.

questions.prompt: Complete: "This box is too heavy for you. Don't worry — I ___ help you with it."

options.text: am help
going to help
will helping
will help

questions.explanation: Offering help in the moment uses will + base verb: I will help you. "will helping" wrongly uses -ing, "going to help" is missing the be (am), and "am help" is not a real form. After will, the verb stays in its base form.

options.text: It's going to rains this evening.
I think they will win the match.
We aren't going to travel this year.
Will you be at home tonight?

questions.explanation: The error is (a): after going to the verb must be the base form — "It's going to rain", not "rains". The third-person -s never appears on a base verb in the future. (b), (c) and (d) are all correct.

questions.prompt: Complete the negative: "A: Is Tom coming? B: No, he ___ come; he changed his plans last week."

options.text: won't to
isn't going to
don't going to
not going to

questions.explanation: A plan that changed earlier is negated with be going to: he isn't going to come. "not going to" is missing the be (isn't), "don't going to" uses the wrong helper, and "won't to" wrongly adds to after won't.

options.text: I'm going to visit my aunt, and I think I will to stay two days.
I will visiting my aunt, and I think I'm going stay two days.
I'm going to visit my aunt, and I think I'll stay two days.
I going to visit my aunt, and I think I will stay to two days.

questions.explanation: Only (c) is fully correct: a planned visit (going to + base verb) plus an opinion (I'll stay). (a) adds a wrong to after will, (b) uses "will visiting" and drops to in "going stay", and (d) drops the be in "I going to" and misplaces to.
